sanskrit_devanagari:
चञ्चलं हि मनः कृष्ण प्रमाथि बलवद्दृढम् ।
तस्याहं निग्रहं मन्ये वायोरिव सुदुष्करम् ॥ ३४ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
cañcalaṁ hi manaḥ kṛṣṇa pramāthi balavad dṛḍham |
tasyāhaṁ nigrahaṁ manye vāyor iva su-duṣkaram || 34 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: The mind is restless, turbulent, obstinate and very strong, O Kṛṣṇa, and to subdue it, I think, is more difficult than controlling the wind.
The mind is very restless, turbulent, strong and obstinate, O Krishna. It appears to me that it is more difficult to control than the wind.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 1817 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The mind is so strong and obstinate that it sometimes overcomes the intelligence, although the mind is supposed to be subservient to the intelligence. For a man in the practical world who has to fight so many opposing elements, it is certainly very difficult to control the mind.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)

disagreements_among_translators.word: vāyor iva su-duṣkaram (very difficult, like the wind)

positions.rendering: I think it is more difficult to control than the wind
harder to control the mind than to control the air outside
as hard as restraining the wind in the sky with one's hands

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All three readings agree the simile names extreme difficulty. Sankara and Ramanuja elaborate the simile in tactile terms; Prab. renders the comparative. The operational reach is shared: the simile communicates the genuineness of the difficulty being named.

literal_meaning: For the mind is restless, O Krishna, churning, strong, obstinate. Restraining it, I consider, is as difficult as restraining the wind.
traditional_meaning_consensus: 6.34 is the full naming of the restless-mind problem. The four adjectives stack: cañcala (unsteady), pramāthi (churning, root math = to churn), balavat (strong), dṛḍham (obstinate). Then the simile: nigraha (restraint) of the mind is like nigraha of the wind — vāyu, the same element used in 6.19's lamp-in-windless-place comparison, now inverted: the mind itself is the wind. Sankara's reading: the four qualities make the mind extremely difficult to subdue; the simile says it is harder to control the mind than to control the air outside. Ramanuja's reading: the mind is naturally fickle, agitates the senses and the body, is powerful and obstinate; restraining it is as hard as restraining the wind in the sky with one's hands. This verse is the most universally recognisable verse in chapter 6 for any practitioner.
